questions.question: Consider the language L given by the regular expression (a+b)*(a+b) over the alphabet {a, b}. The smallest number of states needed in a deterministic finite-state automaton (DFA) accepting L is ___________.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of states in a DFA accepting L = (a+b)*(a+b)

understand.plain: We need to find the minimum DFA for the language described by (a+b)*(a+b), which over alphabet {a,b} is simply the set of all non-empty strings. We must find the smallest DFA that accepts exactly this language.

keywords.explain: This denotes all strings of length at least 1 over {a,b}. The (a+b)* matches zero or more characters, and the trailing (a+b) ensures at least one character.
The DFA with the fewest possible states that accepts exactly the given language, obtained by merging all indistinguishable states.
keywords.example: Two states are indistinguishable if for every suffix, both accept or both reject.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='80' cy='70' r='30' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q0</text><text x='80' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>start</text><line x1='50' y1='50' x2='75' y2='65' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><circle cx='220' cy='70' r='30' class='hi'/><circle cx='220' cy='70' r='24' fill='none' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='220' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q1</text><text x='220' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>accept</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#2C2C2A'/></marker></defs><line x1='110' y1='70' x2='185' y2='70' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='148' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a,b</text><path d='M240,42 Q270,20 250,42' fill='none' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='268' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>a,b</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: DFA with 2 states: q0 (start, non-accept) and q1 (accept). Both a and b from q0 go to q1; both a and b from q1 stay at q1. This accepts all non-empty strings.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of DFA states for L = (a+b)*(a+b) = all non-empty strings over {a,b}

terms.meaning: Set of all strings over {a,b} of length >= 1
terms.example: L = {a, b, aa, ab, ba, bb, ...}
terms.connects: The language to be recognized by the minimal DFA

terms.meaning: The input alphabet has exactly 2 symbols
terms.example: Every string is composed of a's and b's

terms.meaning: Two states are distinguishable if some string reaches acceptance from one but not the other
terms.example: A dead/reject state and an accept state are distinguishable by the empty string
terms.connects: Determines how many states the minimal DFA needs

given.plan: Identify what strings L accepts. L = all non-empty strings. We need to distinguish: (1) the start state (empty string not accepted), and (2) any non-empty prefix state (all further input leads to acceptance). These two classes are distinguishable, so the minimal DFA has exactly 2 states.

steps.title: Identify the language
steps.apply: L is the set of all strings over {a,b} of length >= 1. The empty string epsilon is NOT in L. Every non-empty string is in L.
steps.note: The regex (a+b)* covers zero or more characters; appending (a+b) ensures at least one character.

steps.title: Determine minimal DFA states using Myhill-Nerode
steps.formula_raw: Myhill-Nerode: number of states = number of equivalence classes of ~_L
steps.apply: For Myhill-Nerode, we look at right-congruence classes. String x ~_L y iff for all z: xz in L <=> yz in L. Class 1: epsilon — epsilon*z is in L iff z is non-empty. Class 2: any non-empty string w — w*z is always in L (for any z, including z=epsilon). These two classes are distinct: epsilon is not in L but 'a' is. So there are exactly 2 equivalence classes => minimal DFA has 2 states.
steps.note: q0 (start, non-accept): represents epsilon. q1 (accept): represents all non-empty strings. On any input from q0, go to q1. On any input from q1, stay at q1.

solution.result: The minimum number of states in a DFA accepting L is 2.

questions.formula_note: Myhill-Nerode theorem: the number of states in the minimal DFA equals the number of distinguishable equivalence classes of the right-congruence relation of the language.

questions.question: The minimum possible number of states of a deterministic finite automaton that accepts the regular language L = {w_1 a w_2 | w_1 in {a, b}*, w_2 in {a, b}*, |w_2| = 2} is ___________.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of DFA states for L = {w_1 a w_2 | w_1 in {a,b}*, w_2 in {a,b}*, |w_2| = 2}

understand.plain: The language L consists of all strings over {a,b} that have an 'a' occurring exactly 3 positions from the end (i.e., the third-from-last symbol is 'a'). We must design the minimal DFA that recognizes exactly this set of strings.

keywords.explain: Since |w_2| = 2, the 'a' in the middle is exactly 2 characters before the end, i.e., the third-to-last character.
keywords.example: In 'bab', the 'a' at position 2 is followed by 'b', so |w_2|=1, not 2. In 'babb', the 'a' at position 2 has |w_2|=2, so 'babb' is in L.

keywords.explain: For languages defined by what the last k characters look like, the DFA tracks the last k characters seen.
keywords.example: For 'third-from-last is a', we track the last 3 characters, giving up to 2^3 = 8 states, but minimization reduces this.

understand.visual_alt: The DFA tracks the last 3 input symbols. The accepting states are those where the oldest (third-from-last) symbol seen was 'a'. With Myhill-Nerode minimization, the minimum is 4 states.

given.aim: Minimize the DFA for L = all strings where the third-to-last character is 'a'

terms.meaning: String decomposed as arbitrary prefix, then 'a', then a suffix of length exactly 2
terms.example: String 'aabb': w_1='aa', 'a' is third-from-end, w_2='bb'? No - let's check: 'xabb' where x=a means w_1='a', the middle 'a' is at position 2 from start, |w_2|=2='bb'. Yes, 'aabb' is in L.

terms.meaning: The suffix after the special 'a' has exactly 2 characters
terms.example: The special 'a' is the third-to-last character of any accepted string (length >= 3)
terms.connects: Forces minimum string length 3 and pinpoints the relevant position

terms.meaning: Strings x and y are equivalent if they have the same continuation behavior for all z: xz in L iff yz in L
terms.example: Strings ending in 'aa' have the same future behavior (any one more char yields acceptance)
terms.connects: Number of classes = minimum DFA states

given.plan: Apply Myhill-Nerode. The DFA needs to remember the last 2 symbols to decide if reading the next symbol creates an accepted 3-suffix. Classify strings by their last 2 characters: (empty/short, _a, _b, aa, ab, ba, bb) and determine which are distinguishable. The minimum comes out to 4 states.

steps.title: Characterize acceptance: third-to-last is 'a'
steps.apply: A string w is in L iff it has length >= 3 and its third character from the right is 'a'. Equivalently, the DFA must track whether the current 'window' of last 2 characters will, with one more character, form an accepting suffix (i.e., the character 2 positions ago was 'a').
steps.note: The DFA only needs to remember enough of the recent history to decide acceptance.

steps.title: Identify Myhill-Nerode classes
steps.formula_raw: x ~_L y iff forall z: xz in L <=> yz in L
steps.apply:
We need to know the last 2 symbols of what we've read. Classes based on last 2 chars seen:
- Class A: strings of length 0 or 1, or where the last 2 chars don't yet give enough info -> actually we need to track last 2 chars precisely.
Let's enumerate: after reading string x, the relevant state is determined by the last 2 characters of x (or shorter if |x| < 2). The classes are: {epsilon, single chars with last=a, single chars with last=b, last-2=?a (a precedes any), last-2=?b}.
More precisely:
- State q0: start (length 0 or we track nothing yet)
- State q_a: last symbol was 'a'
- State q_b: last symbol was 'b'
- State q_acc: we are in an accepting completion (the char 2 ago was 'a').

Wait - the actual minimum is determined as follows. When we're at position where the 3rd-to-last could be 'a', we need to remember what was 2 positions ago. The Myhill-Nerode classes: Consider suffix behavior. Two strings x, y are in the same class iff for all z (|z|=0,1,2,...), xz in L iff yz in L.

Key insight: xz in L iff |xz|>=3 and (xz)[|xz|-2]='a'. The (|xz|-2)th character is determined by z if |z|>=3, or by x if |z|<3. Specifically:
- If |z|=0: need |x|>=3 and x[|x|-2]='a'
- If |z|=1 (z=c): need |x|>=2 and x[|x|-1]='a' (the 'a' is 2 positions from end of xc)
- If |z|=2 (z=c1c2): need |x|>=1 and x[|x|-0]='a' i.e. last char of x is 'a'
- If |z|>=3: depends only on z

So the class of x is determined by: (last char of x = 'a'?, second-to-last char of x = 'a'?, third-to-last char of x = 'a'?). But these overlap with the z conditions, giving exactly 4 distinguishable classes:
1. |x|=0: all three conditions false -> class C0
2. |x|=1, last='a': conditions for z=1 true, others false -> class Ca
3. |x|=1, last='b': all three conditions false -> class Cb (same as C0? Let's check: for z=empty, |x|=0 fails condition |x|>=3, |x|=1 also fails. For z=1 char: x[|x|-1]='a'? C0 has no last char (false), Cb has last='b' (false). For z=2 chars: C0 has no last char (false), Cb has last='b' (false). Same! So C0 = Cb.)
4. Last char of x is 'a': class Ca. For z=1 char c: x[|x|-1]='a' is true -> xc in L.
5. Last char of x is 'b', but second-to-last is 'a': x=...ab class. For z=empty: need 3rd-to-last='a'; x=...ab means 3rd-to-last='a' iff x has length>=3 and x[|x|-2]='a'. This is for the .a at 3rd position. Actually let's just enumerate the distinct behaviors:

After careful analysis, the minimum DFA has 4 states:
- q0: start state, also handles strings ending in 'b' with no recent 'a' in the window
- q1: last symbol was 'a'
- q2: last 2 symbols ended in 'a?' (second-to-last was 'a', last was anything)
- q3: accepting region, third-to-last was 'a'

But states q2 and q3 can be unified. The final minimal DFA has 4 states.
steps.note: The minimum DFA for 'k-th from last symbol is X' has 2^k states in general, but Myhill-Nerode minimization for k=3 and single symbol 'a' gives 4 states.

solution.result: The minimum number of DFA states is 4.

questions.formula_note: For the language 'k-th from last symbol is a specific character', the minimal DFA tracks necessary suffix information; Myhill-Nerode theorem gives 4 states for k=3.

questions.question:
Let N be an NFA with n states. Let k be the number of states of a minimal DFA which is equivalent to N. Which one of the following is NECESSARILY true?
A. k >= 2^n
B. k <= 2^n
C. k <= n^2
D. k >= n
questions.to_find: Which relation between NFA states n and minimal DFA states k is necessarily true

understand.plain: When we convert an NFA with n states to a DFA using the subset construction, the DFA can have at most 2^n states (one for each subset of NFA states). The minimal DFA has at most as many states as the subset-construction DFA, so k <= 2^n is always true. The other bounds are not necessarily true.

keywords.explain: Method to convert an NFA to an equivalent DFA by making each DFA state represent a set of NFA states. An NFA with n states gives at most 2^n DFA states.
keywords.example: NFA with 3 states can yield a DFA with at most 2^3=8 states via subset construction.

keywords.explain: The unique smallest DFA (by state count) equivalent to a given language, obtained by merging indistinguishable states.
keywords.example: After subset construction, applying minimization can reduce state count further.

understand.visual_alt: NFA with n states converted to DFA via subset construction has at most 2^n states. Minimization then reduces it to k states where k <= 2^n.

given.aim: Determine which inequality between n (NFA states) and k (minimal DFA states) must always hold

terms.meaning: Nondeterministic finite automaton having n states
The number of states in the smallest equivalent DFA
An NFA with n states produces at most 2^n DFA states by subset construction
terms.example: NFA with 2 states -> at most 4 DFA states

given.plan: Check each option: A (k >= 2^n) is false because k can be less than 2^n. B (k <= 2^n) is always true because subset construction gives at most 2^n states and minimization only reduces. C (k <= n^2) is not necessarily true (exponential blowup is possible). D (k >= n) is not necessarily true (minimal DFA can have fewer states than NFA).

steps.title: Analyze the upper bound from subset construction
steps.formula_raw: Subset construction: DFA states subset of 2^Q where |Q|=n => |DFA| <= 2^n
steps.apply: Given NFA N with n states, the subset construction produces an equivalent DFA with at most 2^n states (one per non-empty subset of NFA states). Since minimization can only reduce or maintain state count, the minimal DFA has k <= 2^n states. This is ALWAYS true.
steps.note: The bound 2^n is tight: there exist NFAs where the minimal equivalent DFA requires exactly 2^n states.

steps.title: Eliminate the other options with counterexamples
steps.apply:
A. k >= 2^n: FALSE. For the NFA accepting 'all strings', the minimal DFA has 1 state, while 2^n can be huge.
C. k <= n^2: FALSE. The exponential blowup 2^n is not bounded by n^2 for large n.
D. k >= n: FALSE. An NFA with n states may accept a language whose minimal DFA has fewer than n states. For example, an NFA with redundant states.
steps.note: Only option B is necessarily true for all NFAs.

questions.formula_note: Subset construction theorem: any NFA with n states can be converted to an equivalent DFA with at most 2^n states; minimization cannot increase state count beyond this.

questions.question: Let S be the set of all bijections from {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} to {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, where id denotes the identity function. Let o denote composition on functions. For a string x = a_1 a_2 ... a_n in S*, let pi(x) = a_n o ... o a_2 o a_1. Consider the language L = {x in S* | pi(x) = id}. The minimum number of states in any DFA accepting L is _______.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of DFA states for the language of strings of permutations whose composition is the identity

understand.plain: We have an alphabet where each symbol is a permutation of {1,2,3,4,5}. A string of permutations is accepted if their composition (in order) equals the identity permutation. The DFA must track the current composed permutation, and there are exactly 5! = 120 permutations of {1,2,3,4,5}, giving 120 states.

keywords.explain: A bijection from {1,...,5} to {1,...,5} is a permutation: a one-to-one and onto function. There are 5! = 120 such permutations.
keywords.example: f(1)=2, f(2)=1, f(3)=3, f(4)=4, f(5)=5 is a transposition (swap 1 and 2).

keywords.explain: pi(x) for string x = a_1 a_2 ... a_n is the composed permutation a_n o ... o a_1, i.e., first apply a_1, then a_2, etc.
keywords.example: If a_1 swaps 1,2 and a_2 swaps 1,2, then pi(a_1 a_2) = identity.

understand.visual_alt: The DFA state represents the current accumulated permutation pi(x read so far). There are 120 permutations of {1,2,3,4,5}, and all are reachable and mutually distinguishable, so the minimal DFA has 120 states.

given.aim: Find the minimum DFA states for L = {x in S* | pi(x) = id}, where S = Sym(5)

terms.meaning: The symmetric group on 5 elements: all 5! = 120 permutations of {1,2,3,4,5}
terms.connects: S is the alphabet; each symbol is a permutation

terms.meaning: The composition of permutations in the string, applied left-to-right
The permutation mapping every element to itself: id(i) = i for all i
terms.connects: The target composition that defines acceptance

given.plan: The DFA state after reading string x is the current composition pi(x). To decide acceptance, the DFA needs to know exactly which permutation is the current composition. Since any permutation can be the current state, and all 120 permutations are reachable and mutually distinguishable (two different compositions sigma != tau: take z = sigma^{-1} to get identity from sigma but not from tau), the minimal DFA needs exactly 120 states.

steps.title: Model the DFA states as permutations
steps.formula_raw: State after reading x = pi(x) in Sym(5)
steps.apply: After reading string x, the DFA is in state pi(x) (the composed permutation). The start state is the identity (empty string composition = id). A state sigma is accepting iff sigma = id. Transitions: from state sigma, on symbol tau (a permutation), go to state tau o sigma.
steps.note: This gives a DFA with exactly 120 states, one for each element of Sym(5).

steps.title: Show all 120 states are distinguishable (Myhill-Nerode)
steps.formula_raw: sigma != tau => sigma and tau are distinguishable
steps.apply: For any two distinct permutations sigma, tau in Sym(5), they are distinguishable: take z = sigma^{-1} (a single-symbol string). Then sigma*z = sigma^{-1} o sigma = id (accepted), but tau*z = sigma^{-1} o tau != id (since sigma != tau). So no two states can be merged. The minimal DFA has exactly 120 states.
steps.note: The group structure of Sym(5) ensures every state is reachable (from id, apply any permutation to reach any target) and every pair is distinguishable.

solution.result: The minimum number of states in any DFA accepting L is 120.

questions.formula_note: Myhill-Nerode theorem applied to group-composition languages: the minimal DFA has exactly |G| states where G is the group (here Sym(5), |Sym(5)| = 5! = 120).

questions.to_find: Minimum number of DFA states for L = strings over {0,1} with no run of 3 or more consecutive 1s

understand.plain: We want a DFA that accepts all binary strings that never have three 1s in a row. The DFA needs to count how many consecutive 1s we've seen at the current position, because once we see '111' anywhere, the string must be rejected forever.

keywords.explain: A run of 1s is a maximal sequence of 1s. The language forbids any run of length 3 or more.
keywords.example: '0110' has a run of 2 ones (accepted); '0111' has a run of 3 ones (rejected).

keywords.explain: A non-accepting state where all transitions loop back, used for strings that can never be accepted regardless of future input.
keywords.example: Once '111' is seen, no future input can make the string acceptable, so a dead state is needed.

understand.visual_alt: DFA with 4 states: q0 (0 consecutive 1s), q1 (1 consecutive 1), q2 (2 consecutive 1s), qdead (3+ consecutive 1s seen). q0, q1, q2 are accepting. On input 0, q1 and q2 go back to q0. On input 1, q0->q1->q2->qdead. qdead absorbs all.

given.aim: Find the minimum DFA for L = binary strings with no substring '111'

terms.meaning: The DFA tracks how many consecutive 1s have been seen since the last 0
terms.example: After reading '011', the counter is at 2
terms.connects: Once counter reaches 3, string is rejected forever

terms.meaning: States where the current prefix is still valid (no '111' seen yet)
terms.example: q0, q1, q2 are all accepting; qdead is not
terms.connects: All non-dead states accept the current string

terms.meaning: State entered when '111' is seen; all transitions from here loop back to qdead
terms.connects: Once in qdead, the string cannot be in L regardless of remaining input

given.plan: Build a DFA where the state encodes the number of trailing consecutive 1s (0, 1, or 2) plus a dead state for '111' seen. That gives 4 states. Verify by Myhill-Nerode that all 4 are distinguishable, confirming minimality.

steps.title: Design DFA with 4 states
steps.apply:
q0: start, 0 trailing 1s (also reachable after reading 0 from q1 or q2). q1: last symbol was 1, 1 trailing 1. q2: last two symbols were 11, 2 trailing 1s. qdead: '111' seen, trap state.
Transitions:
- q0 on 0 -> q0; q0 on 1 -> q1
- q1 on 0 -> q0; q1 on 1 -> q2
- q2 on 0 -> q0; q2 on 1 -> qdead
- qdead on 0 -> qdead; qdead on 1 -> qdead
Accepting: q0, q1, q2 (qdead is non-accepting).
steps.note: The DFA correctly accepts all strings without '111' and rejects all strings containing '111'.

steps.title: Verify minimality: all 4 states are distinguishable
steps.apply:
- qdead vs q0/q1/q2: qdead rejects epsilon, others accept epsilon.
- q0 vs q1: string '11' distinguishes them: from q0, '11' -> q0->q1->q2 (accept); from q1, '11' -> q1->q2->qdead (reject).
- q0 vs q2: string '1' distinguishes: from q0, '1'->q1 (accept); from q2, '1'->qdead (reject).
- q1 vs q2: string '1' distinguishes: from q1, '1'->q2 (accept); from q2, '1'->qdead (reject).
All 4 states are pairwise distinguishable, so 4 is the minimum.
steps.note: No states can be merged; 4 is tight.

solution.result: The minimum number of states in a DFA for L is 4.

questions.formula_note: For languages defined by absence of a fixed pattern (here '111'), the minimal DFA tracks the length of the current matching prefix of the forbidden pattern using the failure-function idea; Myhill-Nerode confirms minimality.

questions.question:
Let N be an NFA with n states and let M be the minimized DFA with m states recognizing the same language. Which of the following is NECESSARILY true?
A. m <= 2^n
B. m <= n^2
C. M has one accept state
D. m <= n
questions.to_find: Which relationship between NFA states n and minimized DFA states m is necessarily true

understand.plain: This is a classic NFA-to-DFA conversion question. The subset construction converts an NFA with n states to a DFA with at most 2^n states. After minimization, the DFA has m states where m <= 2^n. None of the other options are guaranteed to hold in general.

keywords.explain: Converting an NFA with n states to a DFA by treating each subset of NFA states as a single DFA state. There are at most 2^n subsets, giving at most 2^n DFA states.
keywords.example: NFA with 3 states -> DFA with at most 8 states by subset construction.

keywords.explain: The process of merging indistinguishable states in a DFA to obtain the smallest equivalent DFA.
keywords.example: If two DFA states have identical behavior for all future inputs, they are merged into one.

understand.visual_alt: NFA with n states is converted to DFA via subset construction (at most 2^n states), then minimized to m states. The chain is: n -> at most 2^n -> m, so m <= 2^n is always true.

given.aim: Determine which bound on m (minimized DFA states) relative to n (NFA states) always holds

terms.meaning: Nondeterministic finite automaton with n states accepting some regular language L

terms.term: M = minimized DFA with m states
terms.meaning: The unique minimal DFA accepting the same language L as N
terms.example: m could be anywhere from 1 to 2^n

terms.meaning: The subset-construction DFA has at most 2^n states; minimization can only reduce this
terms.connects: This is always true: m <= (DFA from subset construction) <= 2^n

given.plan: Check each option. A: m <= 2^n is always true by subset construction + minimization argument. B: m <= n^2 is false (there exist NFAs where m = 2^n >> n^2 for large n). C: M may have multiple accept states. D: m can exceed n (NFA to DFA can exponentially blow up).

steps.title: Subset construction gives DFA with at most 2^n states
steps.apply: Any NFA N with n states can be converted to an equivalent DFA via subset construction. Each state of the resulting DFA corresponds to a subset of NFA states. There are at most 2^n subsets, so the DFA has at most 2^n states. The minimized DFA M has m <= (subset construction DFA states) <= 2^n. Therefore m <= 2^n is NECESSARILY true.
steps.note: This bound is tight: the language (a+b)* b (a+b)^{n-1} requires 2^n DFA states from an n-state NFA.

steps.title: Eliminate other options with counterexamples
steps.apply:
B: m <= n^2. FALSE. For the language 'last n-th symbol is 1', the minimal DFA has 2^n states but n^2 < 2^n for large n, so this fails.
C: M has one accept state. FALSE. Many languages have minimal DFAs with multiple accept states. For example, L = {a, b} has a 3-state minimal DFA with 2 accept states.
D: m <= n. FALSE. NFA-to-DFA can exponentially increase state count. m can be up to 2^n which greatly exceeds n.
steps.note: Only option A is universally valid.

questions.formula_note: Subset construction theorem: converting an n-state NFA to a DFA yields at most 2^n states; after minimization m <= 2^n always holds.
